steps.title: Apply the dangling modifier rule
steps.content: The sentence opens with "Having 11,486 feet of prominence," — an introductory participial phrase. Whatever follows the comma must be the grammatical subject that the phrase describes. Only a mountain can logically possess topographical prominence.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — geographers have ranked Mount Hayes, a peak in the United States, as the world's 51st most prominent mountain.: "Geographers" is the subject. Geographers don't have 11,486 feet of prominence — dangling modifier. ✗
  • Choice B — Mount Hayes, a peak located in the United States, is ranked by geographers as the world's 51st most prominent mountain.: "Mount Hayes" is the subject. The mountain has the prominence — the modifier correctly describes the subject that follows the comma. ✓
  • Choice C — the list of mountains geographers rank as the world's most prominent includes Mount Hayes, a peak located in the United States, at number 51.: "The list" is the subject. A list doesn't have topographical prominence — dangling modifier. ✗
  • Choice D — the ranking given by geographers to Mount Hayes, a peak located in the United States, is 51st most prominent mountain in the world.: "The ranking" is the subject. Rankings don't have prominence — dangling modifier. ✗
B is correct.
